body:
Dear [Hiring Manager],
A tool I built over three weekends now has 4,200 active users and a 4.8 rating. I wrote it because an everyday workflow annoyed me, and building the fix taught me more than any course I took that year.
I brought the same approach to my internship at [Company], where I rebuilt a data pipeline that processed 1.2 million records a day. The previous version took 4.1 hours and failed about once a week. Mine runs in 97 minutes and has not failed in six months, and it cut cloud cost by roughly 4,200 dollars a month. I proposed it in my second week after reading the error logs, and I owned it through to production. I also wrote the tests and the runbook for it, because a pipeline no one else can operate is a liability rather than an asset, and I would rather hand over something a teammate can maintain without me.
I graduate from [University] in [Month] with a First in Computer Science. Across three internships I have shipped production code in Python, TypeScript, and Go, and one reviewer described my pull requests as unusually well-structured for a junior engineer. I am most useful when I can see how a whole system fits together and then make one part of it measurably better.
Your engineering blog post on event-driven services for the recommendation system covers a problem I studied closely in my final-year thesis, and I reached a similar conclusion about why that design holds up under load. I would like the chance to work on that kind of problem with your team rather than on my own.
My GitHub, linked below, includes both projects with live deployments and the thesis. I would welcome a technical conversation whenever suits you. I learn fastest with people who care about the craft, and everything I have read about your team suggests that is exactly the environment you have built.
Sincerely,
[Your Name]  |  [Phone]  |  [Email]  |  github.com/[handle]

Dear [Hiring Manager],
I joined [Previous Company] as one of three engineers and left as technical lead of eight. In between, the platform grew from 50,000 to 1.4 million monthly users, and I made or reviewed most of the architectural decisions that had to survive that growth.
A few of those decisions paid off clearly. A Redis caching layer cut p99 API latency by 73 percent. A rebuilt test suite brought CI times down from 22 minutes to 4. A feature-flag system let us release to 5 percent of users, measure, and adjust without deployment risk. One decision did not age well: I extracted a service too early and it cost four weeks to undo. That month taught me more about system boundaries than the year before it. What I take from all of it is that the value of a senior engineer is mostly in the decisions that never become incidents, the boring choices about boundaries and data ownership that quietly keep a system healthy as it grows.
My core stack is TypeScript and Python, with production experience in Go where the problem called for it. I am comfortable across system design, performance work, and the developer-experience improvements that let the people around me move faster. I also care about the craft of the codebase itself: clear naming, tests that document intent, and the kind of internal tooling that makes the next engineer faster.
Your blog describes a principle you call boring infrastructure, exciting products. That matches how I prefer to work, and your service-mesh migration series stood out for the care taken over rollback safety and sequencing rather than novelty for its own sake.
My CV and portfolio are attached. I am happy to walk through any decision in detail, including the ones I would now make differently. I am looking for a team where engineering quality is treated as a feature rather than an afterthought, and your writing suggests you are that team.
Best regards,
[Your Name]  |  [Phone]  |  [Email]

Dear [Hiring Manager],
The last system I designed holds 340,000 concurrent connections at a p99 latency under 12 milliseconds. The one it replaced capped near 18,000 and fell over most Tuesdays. Closing that kind of gap between what a system is and what it should be is the work I have spent eight years getting good at.
At [Company] I am the technical owner of a platform that processes 2.1 billion dollars in transactions a year. Recent work includes a fraud-detection service that cut false positives by 44 percent and saved about 1.8 million dollars in annual chargebacks, a zero-downtime migration across 14 dependent services, and a set of engineering standards that became company policy and reduced production incidents by 67 percent year over year. I hold firm, data-backed views on observability, data contracts, and the moments in a system's life when early abstraction does the most harm. The pattern across all three was the same: most production pain comes not from the hard problems people expect but from the ordinary ones no one owned, and a large part of my job is making sure those have an owner before they bite.
I work in Go, Python, and Java, and I can contribute to Rust where its performance characteristics justify the cost of adopting it.
The adaptive load-shedding approach your team published in 2023 addresses a problem I met at smaller scale in 2021. My answer then was less elegant than yours, and I have kept thinking about the difference since. I would like to bring that thinking, and the production lessons behind it, to your team.
I look forward to a technical conversation and am glad to talk through any of the decisions above, including the two I would approach differently today. I am at the stage of my career where I want harder problems and stronger colleagues, and your platform offers both.
Regards,
[Your Name]  |  [Phone]  |  [Email]  |  linkedin.com/in/[handle]

hookDesc: changing the questions the org asks
body:
Dear [Hiring Manager],
The clearest measure of a principal engineer is not the code they write but the questions the organisation starts asking because of them. I have changed those questions twice. At [Company A] an internal developer platform I led brought mean time to production down from eleven days to four hours across 200 engineers. At [Company B] an RFC I wrote became the basis of a platform strategy that retired 6 million dollars of legacy infrastructure and freed two product teams to ship faster than they had in three years.
My value is technical and organisational in equal measure. I have set distributed-tracing standards now used across seven business units, written proposals that turned into product lines, and argued in both engineering forums and executive meetings for the investments that were hardest to justify and returned the most. I am as comfortable at the whiteboard as in the boardroom, and I translate between them. In both cases the technical change was the easy part, and the real work was building the consensus across teams that made the change stick after I moved on to the next problem.
My technical depth runs through systems design and cloud-native infrastructure. My strategic depth is mostly about sequencing: how to order technical investments so they compound instead of conflict.
The acquisition of [Target Company] you announced in [Month] creates exactly the platform-consolidation problem I find most interesting. Several integration architectures will get serious consideration, but only one will still look right in three years. I have a clear view on which, and why.
I am happy to share the [Company B] RFC as an example of how I think and reason. I look forward to speaking with your VP of Engineering. I am looking for a place where the technical strategy genuinely shapes the business strategy, and the scale of your ambition suggests this is one.
Respectfully,
[Your Name]  |  [Phone]  |  [Email]

Dear [Hiring Manager],
On my own initiative last year I ran 87 user interviews about invoicing. The pattern underneath them was not a missing feature but a missing feeling: small business owners did not trust their software to represent them well. I rebuilt the information hierarchy around that insight, and 60-day retention moved from 12 percent to 54 percent.
I am completing my MBA at [Business School] after two years as a product intern at [Company A] and [Company B]. At [Company B] I led the discovery work for a B2B onboarding flow that brought time-to-value down from 14 days to 3, with no engineering sprint until the design had been validated across five structured user sessions. I write clear PRDs, run disciplined discovery, and test assumptions with data before they harden into requirements. I am comfortable being the person who pushes a team to talk to ten more users before committing to a build, because the cost of that week is always smaller than the cost of shipping the wrong thing for a quarter.
Your CPO made a point at [Conference] that matches how I work: the worst product decisions come from skipping discovery and going straight to a solution. I have applied that more times than I can count, including the times it told me to stop building something I liked.
As a daily user of your product, I have written down 23 hypotheses I would want to research in my first 90 days. I am less interested in talking about features than about the problems those features exist to solve.
My portfolio and the research synthesis from my most recent internship are attached. I look forward to the conversation. I would rather earn the role through how I think about a real problem of yours than through a list of credentials, and I am happy to do exactly that.
Best,
[Your Name]  |  [Phone]  |  [Email]

Dear [Hiring Manager],
As Product Lead at [Company] I took one feature from concept to 11 million dollars in incremental annual revenue over three years. That figure came from careful opportunity sizing, a cross-functional model that kept engineering, design, and sales aligned across an 18-month roadmap, and the discipline to kill three reasonable ideas that would have diluted focus. I want to apply those habits at larger scale.
My background is in B2B SaaS, with real depth in pricing and packaging, product-led growth instrumentation, and platform monetisation. I have owned roadmaps for products used by more than 2,000 enterprise clients, run discovery with senior buyers, and built the analytics that gave my teams honest clarity on activation, retention, and expansion. Engineering leads have called my PRDs the most useful documents they read, which I take as the highest compliment available to a PM. I am equally comfortable saying no to a senior stakeholder when the data does not support their idea, which I have found is most of what separates a roadmap that compounds from one that simply grows longer. I also rebuilt the way we sized opportunities, replacing optimistic top-down guesses with bottom-up models the sales team could actually stand behind in a forecast.
Your move into the mid-market is the kind of problem I am built for. It calls for sharper segmentation, a packaging architecture that serves both sophistication and simplicity, and a different definition of success than the enterprise motion uses. I have made this transition before and have a specific view on the order in which to do it.
I am available this week or next. I am glad to bring a short case study of the 11 million dollar initiative to show how I work through ambiguity. I am looking for the next stage where these habits operate at greater scale, and the trajectory of your product suggests it is here.
Regards,
[Your Name]  |  [Phone]  |  [Email]

hookDesc: model that beat a commercial baseline
body:
Dear [Hiring Manager],
My final-year model forecast demand 18.4 points better on MAPE than the retailer's existing system. The number I am prouder of is the two-page summary I wrote so the buying team could act on it without a statistics background. The hardest part of this work is rarely the model. It is the translation.
I graduate from [University] in Statistics with a focus on machine learning, after internships at [Company A] and [Company B]. At [Company B] I built an A/B testing framework from scratch that tripled the team's experiment velocity and is still in use. I work in Python, SQL, dbt, and Airflow, and I start every analysis with one question: what decision will this inform? I am also the kind of analyst who documents their assumptions clearly, because a model no one else can interrogate is a black box, and a black box is the fastest way to lose a stakeholder's trust. I have also learned to scope an analysis to the decision in front of it, because the most elegant model is wasted effort if it answers a question no one is going to act on.
What draws me to [Company] is structural. Your data team sits inside the product organisation rather than in a separate centre of excellence. That choice puts analysts close to the decisions they support, and it is the environment I want to learn in.
My portfolio is at [URL] and includes the forecasting model, two production dashboards, and the testing framework. I would welcome a conversation about your current analytical priorities. I want to build my career somewhere analysts are close to real decisions, and the way your team is structured tells me this is that place.
Best regards,
[Your Name]  |  [Phone]  |  [Email]

Dear [Hiring Manager],
A recommendation engine I designed serves 180 million daily impressions under 40 milliseconds. A fraud-scoring system I built handles 9,000 transactions a second. A credit-risk model I validated cut default rates by 22 percent at a major lender. I list these together because they span very different deployment contexts, and the lesson I value most is that a practice which works in one of them can quietly fail in another.
Seven years of production machine learning have given me solid MLOps capability alongside the modelling. I have built feature stores, designed monitoring that catches distribution shift before it reaches business metrics, and led platform work that brought deployment cycles down from six weeks to four days. I publish selectively, with two papers in applied NLP, and I read widely. I hold clear views on interpretability in regulated settings and on the point where added model complexity stops earning its keep. I treat monitoring and rollback as first-class parts of any model I ship, because in production the question is never only whether a model is accurate today but whether you will notice the day it quietly stops being so. I have also built the data and labelling pipelines that quietly determine whether a model works at all, because the modelling is only ever as good as the inputs feeding it.
I work in Python with PyTorch and JAX, and deploy in Go. I have designed for both real-time and petabyte-scale batch contexts.
Your work on contextual bandits for personalisation, and the patent filed in the second quarter, reflects the production-first, theory-aware approach I respect. I have specific ideas about the cold-start problem you likely meet at the tail of your user distribution, and I would rather show you those ideas than describe them here.
I look forward to a conversation with your ML leadership team. I am looking for a team that treats production reliability and research ambition as the same discipline, and your work suggests you do.
Sincerely,
[Your Name]  |  [Phone]  |  [Email]  |  scholar.google.com/[handle]

Dear [Hiring Manager],
Over five years my work has moved real numbers. A checkout redesign lifted mobile conversion by 31 percent. An onboarding flow cut time to first value from 11 minutes to 3. An accessibility overhaul raised NPS by 18 points, because people who can use a product come to trust it.
My process starts with research, not pixels. At [Previous Company] I ran more than 60 user interviews across three markets, turned the findings into a jobs-to-be-done framework, and used it to reduce the number of distinct UI patterns in our design system from 94 to 31. The product became more consistent and faster to build on at the same time. Engineers have told me my specs are the reason they do not have to guess. I work in Figma, prototype in Framer, and build component libraries that outlast my time on a team. I am comfortable defending a design decision with research rather than taste, and equally comfortable abandoning my own preferred direction when a usability test shows it is the wrong one, which happens more often than any designer likes to admit.
I have studied your design system closely. The spacing rules and motion guidelines in your public documentation show unusual restraint, a clear refusal to let the system sprawl. Your mobile-first initiative sits in some tension with the current grid, and I have three concrete approaches to resolving it. I also write the kind of documentation that lets a design survive handoff intact, because a beautiful prototype that engineers have to reinterpret is a design half-finished.
My portfolio is at [URL]. I would rather walk you through the reasoning behind two case studies than have you read them cold. I would rather contribute to a system built with this much intent than start one from scratch elsewhere.
Best,
[Your Name]  |  [Phone]  |  [Email]  |  [Portfolio URL]

hookDesc: a clear view of the technical challenge
body:
Dear [Board Chair / CEO],
The question in front of [Company] is whether your engineering organisation can scale from a strong product company into a durable platform business. I have led that exact transition twice, and I have a specific view on how you would do it.
At [Company A] I grew an engineering team from 40 to 260 while improving deployment frequency tenfold and bringing change-failure rate below 5 percent. At [Company B] I rebuilt the technical organisation after a difficult merger, consolidated three overlapping platforms into one, and protected delivery throughout, cutting infrastructure cost by 31 percent without a single missed release. My mandate in both was the same: align technical strategy with commercial outcomes and make engineering a reason customers stay rather than a cost centre. I am candid with a board about trade-offs rather than promising everything on every timeline, because the fastest way to lose an engineering organisation's trust is to commit it to dates that the people doing the work know are fiction.
I lead through clarity rather than heroics. That means hiring well, setting standards that hold without me in the room, and being honest in the executive team about what technology can and cannot do on a given timeline. I have owned budgets above 40 million dollars and reported to boards on security, technical risk, and capital allocation.
Your recent platform announcement implies an architecture decision that will shape the next five years. I have lived through the version of that decision that compounds well and the version that quietly accrues cost, and I can tell the difference early.
I would welcome a conversation with the board about the first hundred days and where the leverage truly sits. I am looking for a company at exactly this inflection, where the engineering decisions of the next year set the ceiling for the next five.
Regards,
[Your Name]  |  [Phone]  |  [Email]

hookDesc: a model that caught a real error
body:
Dear [Hiring Manager],
During my internship at [Company] I rebuilt a revenue model the team had used for two years and found a double-counting error that had overstated a forecast by 1.3 million dollars. I raised it carefully, showed the workings, and the corrected model became the one the team now uses. I learned that precision is a form of respect for the people who rely on your numbers.
I graduate from [University] in [Month] with a degree in Finance and a near-perfect record in my accounting and valuation modules. I am fluent in Excel to a level that includes building three-statement models and dynamic DCFs from scratch, and I am comfortable in SQL and Python for handling larger datasets. In a stock-pitch competition I placed first of 40 teams with a long thesis that has since outperformed its benchmark. I am the kind of analyst who reconciles to the source rather than trusting the spreadsheet, because in finance the small unchecked assumption is exactly where the expensive mistakes hide. I also taught myself to automate the repetitive parts of the reporting in Python during my internship, which freed the team to spend its time on judgement rather than copy-paste.
I want to start my career at [Company] specifically because of your reputation for analytical rigour and for developing analysts who can think, not just produce. Your recent commentary on capital discipline in the sector matches how I already try to approach valuation.
My CV and a sample valuation model are attached. I would welcome the chance to discuss how I would contribute to your team. I want to learn this craft somewhere the standard is genuinely high, and your reputation tells me yours is.
Sincerely,
[Your Name]  |  [Phone]  |  [Email]

Dear [Hiring Manager],
The clearest measure of financial analysis is whether it changes a decision. My scenario model on a proposed acquisition at [Company] showed the deal destroyed value below a revenue-synergy threshold the deal team had treated as conservative. The board paused, renegotiated, and closed at a price 14 percent lower. The analysis paid for my salary several times over in one meeting.
Across eight years in FP&A and corporate finance I have owned annual planning for a 600 million dollar business unit, built the forecasting model that reduced variance between guidance and actuals from 9 percent to under 3, and partnered with operating leaders who came to treat finance as a thinking partner rather than a scorekeeper. I work fluently in Excel, SQL, Power BI, and increasingly Python, and I care as much about how a number is communicated as how it is derived. I am as comfortable presenting a difficult finding to a sceptical operating leader as I am building the model behind it, because analysis that no one acts on is just expensive arithmetic. I also rebuilt the monthly reporting so leaders could see the story in the numbers in two minutes rather than twenty, because analysis no one has time to read changes nothing.
What interests me about [Company] is the move from a single product line into a portfolio, which changes how capital should be allocated and measured. I have built the allocation framework for exactly that shift before and have a clear view of the traps in it.
I am happy to walk through the acquisition model or the planning rebuild in detail. I look forward to speaking with you. I want to do this work somewhere the analysis is actually used to decide, and everything about your team suggests it is.
Regards,
[Your Name]  |  [Phone]  |  [Email]

Dear [Hiring Manager],
In my three years as an analyst at [Bank] I worked on 11 closed transactions worth a combined 8.2 billion dollars, including a cross-border acquisition where the buyer's financing fell through nine days before signing. I rebuilt the model overnight to reflect three alternative structures, and the structure I recommended is the one that closed.
That pace and that standard are now second nature. I have built merger models, LBO models, and DCFs that managing directors took into client meetings without changes, drafted the analytical sections of pitch books for clients up to 40 billion dollars in enterprise value, and managed the data room for two sell-side processes from launch to close. I am known on my team for catching the error in the appendix that no one else read. I work well under the hours this job demands without letting the quality slip in hour fourteen, because a single transposed figure in a client model can undo a month of careful work, and I have seen what that costs. I am also the analyst who reads the fine print in the agreements, because in a transaction the detail buried in a schedule is often the thing that decides whether the model holds.
I am applying to [Bank] because of your franchise in [sector] and your reputation for putting associates on live execution early rather than on the bench. Your role in the [recent named transaction] is the kind of work I want to be building toward.
My CV is attached, and I am happy to discuss any of the transactions above within the bounds of confidentiality. I look forward to your response. I am ready for the pace and the standard, and I want to learn it on a desk with your reputation for execution.
Sincerely,
[Your Name]  |  [Phone]  |  [Email]

Dear [Board Chair / CEO],
A CFO is hired to answer one question well: where does this company create value, and where does it merely consume capital? I have answered it twice through difficult transitions, and I believe [Company] is at a similar inflection now.
At [Company A] I led the finance function through a period that took revenue from 180 million to 620 million dollars, took the company public, and held gross margin steady through the scale. At [Company B] I restructured a balance sheet that had become a constraint, refinanced 300 million dollars of debt on materially better terms, and built the planning discipline that let the board allocate capital with confidence rather than instinct. I treat investor communication, treasury, and operational finance as one connected system, because that is how the market reads them. I am the kind of CFO who would rather deliver a conservative number I can defend than an optimistic one I have to walk back, because credibility with the market is built slowly and lost in a single missed quarter.
I lead a finance organisation that operating partners want in the room. That comes from honest numbers delivered on time, a team developed deliberately, and a refusal to let optimism quietly enter a forecast.
Your recent guidance and the capital structure behind it suggest a specific set of choices in the next 18 months about where to invest and where to hold. I have a clear point of view on the sequencing, and I would value the chance to test it against the board's own.
I would welcome a conversation about the first hundred days. I am looking for a board and a chief executive who want a finance partner in the strategy rather than a scorekeeper after it, and your stage suggests you do.
Regards,
[Your Name]  |  [Phone]  |  [Email]

hookDesc: an investment view that proved out
body:
Dear [Hiring Manager],
At [Bank] I built the operating model for a take-private that I argued was mispriced because the market was treating a one-off working-capital swing as structural. The fund that pursued it has since marked the position up 2.3 times. Being early and right, and being able to show the work behind it, is the part of this job I am drawn to.
My three years in [sector] M&A gave me the modelling and diligence foundation the associate seat demands: full LBO models built from first principles, commercial and financial diligence across nine processes, and the quality-of-earnings work that separates a clean deal from a problem hiding in the footnotes. I read management teams as carefully as I read their numbers, because the plan only matters if the people can execute it. I read a management team as carefully as I read their accounts, because a sound thesis executed by the wrong people is still a loss, and most of the deals that disappoint were visible in the diligence if you were willing to see them. I also build the operating model from the bottom up rather than inheriting management's, because the gap between the two is usually where the real return, or the real risk, is hiding.
I am applying to [Firm] specifically because of your operational approach to value creation rather than financial engineering alone, and because your focus on [named sector] matches where I have built genuine pattern recognition. Your investment in [named portfolio company] is a clear example of the thesis I want to help underwrite.
My CV is attached. I would welcome the chance to discuss your current pipeline and how I would contribute to it. I want to underwrite theses I believe in alongside people who pressure-test them honestly, and your team has that reputation.
Sincerely,
[Your Name]  |  [Phone]  |  [Email]

hookDesc: a loss prevented, not just measured
body:
Dear [Hiring Manager],
Good risk management is mostly invisible, which makes it hard to value until the moment it matters. In 2022 my team flagged a concentration in the lending book that the front office considered acceptable. We held the line, reduced the exposure over two quarters, and avoided an estimated 24 million dollars in losses when that sector turned the following year. Being unpopular for the right reasons is part of the role.
Over ten years across market and credit risk I have built the stress-testing framework a regulator later cited as best practice, brought a value-at-risk model into line with Basel requirements without overstating capital, and rebuilt the relationship between risk and the trading desk so that risk was treated as a partner rather than a brake. I am fluent in the quantitative side and equally comfortable explaining a tail scenario to a board in plain language. I have learned that the hardest part of risk is not the modelling but the conversation, holding a position the desk dislikes while keeping the relationship intact, because a risk function the business routes around protects no one. I also rebuilt the way risk was reported to the board, replacing dense tables with a clear read on the three exposures that actually mattered that quarter.
What interests me about [Company] is the build-out of your [named business line], which introduces risks your current framework was not designed for. I have stood up a risk function for a new line before and know where the early gaps tend to hide.
I would welcome a conversation about your current risk priorities and where I could add the most value. I want to build risk where it is treated as a genuine partner to the business, and your stated approach suggests it is.
Regards,
[Your Name]  |  [Phone]  |  [Email]

hookDesc: a campaign that outperformed its budget
body:
Dear [Hiring Manager],
During my internship at [Company] I was handed a 2,000 dollar budget for a student-focused campaign that previous interns had treated as a formality. I rebuilt it around three creators whose audiences actually matched ours, and the campaign drove 1,400 sign-ups at a cost per acquisition 60 percent below the company's paid-social average. The budget was small, so every decision had to earn its place.
I graduate from [University] in [Month] with a degree in Marketing. Alongside my studies I grew a personal account in [niche] to 22,000 followers by testing content weekly and keeping what worked, which taught me more about audiences than any single module. I am comfortable in Google Analytics, Meta Ads Manager, and the basics of SQL, and I can write copy that sounds like a person rather than a brand template. I treat every budget as if the money were my own, which is why I would rather run one campaign properly than spread a small budget across five that each do too little to measure. I also learned to read the analytics rather than guess, tracking each channel honestly so the next campaign could build on what actually worked rather than on what felt successful.
I want to work at [Company] because your campaigns treat the audience as intelligent. Your recent [named campaign] made a real argument instead of chasing a trend, and that is the kind of marketing I want to learn to make.
My portfolio, including the internship campaign and my own account analytics, is attached. I would welcome the chance to talk. I want to learn marketing somewhere it is treated as a craft rather than a cost, and your work tells me it is.
Best,
[Your Name]  |  [Phone]  |  [Email]

Dear [Hiring Manager],
Most growth marketing buys revenue at a loss and calls it momentum. At [Company] I grew paid acquisition revenue by 140 percent over two years while improving return on ad spend from 2.1 to 3.8, because I treated the budget as if it were my own money.
My work spans paid search and social, lifecycle and email, and the analytics that tie them together. I rebuilt an attribution model that had been crediting the last click for everything, which changed where we spent and lifted blended efficiency by 28 percent. I ran more than 300 structured experiments in three years and killed the ones that did not earn their place quickly, including several I had personally championed. I work in Google Ads, Meta, GA4, and increasingly in SQL so I can answer my own questions rather than wait in a queue. I would rather kill a campaign that is not working in week two than defend it for a quarter out of pride, because the budget belongs to the business, not to my ego, and the discipline to stop is rarer than the creativity to start.
What interests me about [Company] is your shift toward retention as a growth lever rather than pure acquisition, which is the harder and more durable game. I have built the lifecycle program that makes that shift work and have a clear view of where to start.
I am happy to share the attribution rebuild and the experiment framework as examples of how I work. I look forward to speaking with you. I want to do this work somewhere efficiency and growth are held to the same account, and your shift toward retention tells me you do.
Regards,
[Your Name]  |  [Phone]  |  [Email]

Dear [Hiring Manager],
When I took over the [product] brand it was growing slower than the category and competing almost entirely on price. Over three years I repositioned it around a single clear idea, lifted unprompted brand awareness from 31 to 58 percent, and grew revenue by 47 percent while reducing promotional discounting. Brand work is slow, and the discipline is in resisting the pressure to chase a quick number.
My background combines the creative and the commercial. I have run repositionings backed by real consumer research, managed agency relationships that produced work the business was proud of, and owned a P&L of 90 million dollars. I am as comfortable defending a creative idea in a boardroom as I am reading a brand-tracking study, and I believe the best brand decisions sit at the intersection of the two. I am comfortable holding a brand position steady against the quarterly pressure to discount, because brand equity is built over years and squandered in a single panicked promotion, and protecting it is most of the job. I also rebuilt the relationship between brand and the commercial team, so brand decisions were made with the P&L in view rather than in spite of it.
What draws me to [Company] is that you are at the point many brands reach and few navigate well: strong recognition, but a younger audience that does not yet see itself in the brand. I have led that exact generational handover and know how to do it without alienating the customers who built the business.
I would welcome a conversation about where you want the brand to be in five years and how to get there. I want to build a brand somewhere patience is allowed, and your portfolio suggests you understand that brand is a long game.
Best regards,
[Your Name]  |  [Phone]  |  [Email]

hookDesc: marketing as a growth engine, accountably
body:
Dear [CEO],
A CMO earns their seat by making marketing accountable for growth rather than for activity. I have built marketing organisations that the rest of the executive team trusted with revenue, and I believe [Company] is ready for that shift.
At [Company A] I led marketing through a period that took the business from 80 million to 240 million dollars in revenue, building the demand engine, the brand, and the analytics that connected spend to pipeline to closed revenue. At [Company B] I rebuilt a function that had become a service desk for sales into a strategic driver, and we cut customer acquisition cost by 34 percent while doubling qualified pipeline. I treat brand and performance as one system, because customers do not experience them as separate. I am candid with the board about what marketing can and cannot influence on a given timeline, because the fastest way to lose a marketing budget is to over-promise on attribution and then fail to defend it when finance asks the hard question.
I lead by giving teams a clear strategy and the freedom to execute it, and by being honest with the board about what marketing can and cannot move on a given timeline. I have owned budgets above 30 million dollars and reported on marketing's contribution in the same terms the finance team uses.
Your category is consolidating, which usually rewards the brand that defines the conversation rather than the one that spends the most. I have a specific view on how [Company] could take that position, and on the first three moves that would matter most.
I would welcome a conversation about the opportunity. I am looking for a chief executive who wants marketing held to the same standard as the rest of the business, and your stage suggests you are.
Regards,
[Your Name]  |  [Phone]  |  [Email]

hookDesc: content that drove pipeline, not vanity metrics
body:
Dear [Hiring Manager],
Most content programs measure traffic because traffic is easy to grow and hard to argue with. At [Company] I built a content strategy measured against pipeline instead, and over 18 months organic content became the source of 38 percent of new sales-qualified leads at roughly a third the cost of paid.
I work where editorial judgement meets commercial intent. I have built editorial calendars anchored to the questions buyers actually ask, commissioned and edited work from specialist writers, and developed the search strategy that put our content in front of people at the moment they were deciding. I write well myself, but my real value is shaping a body of work so it compounds, where each piece makes the next one easier to rank and more useful to read. I am happy to be measured against pipeline rather than traffic, because traffic is easy to grow and hard to bank, and a content program that cannot show its contribution to revenue is always first on the list when budgets tighten. I also built the measurement framework that connected each piece of content to pipeline, because a content team that cannot prove its contribution is always the first budget cut in a hard quarter.
What interests me about [Company] is that you sell a genuinely complex product, which is the setting where good content has the most leverage, because buyers need to understand before they can buy. Your recent [named piece] showed a willingness to teach rather than sell, and that is the foundation I would build on.
My portfolio and the pipeline analysis behind the strategy above are attached. I look forward to the conversation. I want to build a content program somewhere teaching is valued over selling, and your recent work tells me it is.
Best,
[Your Name]  |  [Phone]  |  [Email]

hookDesc: outbound results against the team average
body:
Dear [Hiring Manager],
In my first nine months in an SDR role I booked 41 qualified meetings a quarter against a team average of 22, and I did it with a reply rate well above the floor because I refused to send the templates everyone else was sending. I researched each account properly and wrote like a person. It took longer per email and worked far better.
Before sales I spent two years in hospitality, which taught me more about reading people and staying steady under pressure than any course could. I am organised enough to manage a large pipeline without letting anyone slip, and I am comfortable in Salesforce, Outreach, and the research tools that make outbound personal rather than mechanical. Rejection does not rattle me, because I treat each no as information about who to talk to next. I keep my pipeline honest in the CRM rather than flattering it, because a forecast built on wishful stages helps no one, least of all me when the quarter closes and the meetings were never real. I also keep meticulous notes on every account so that no conversation starts from scratch, because the difference between a booked meeting and a missed one is often a single detail remembered.
I want to join [Company] because you sell to a buyer I find genuinely interesting and because your team has a reputation for developing reps into closers rather than burning them out. Your recent growth in [named segment] is exactly the wave I want to be selling into.
I would welcome the chance to show you how I research an account and how I would approach your ideal customer. My CV is attached. I want to learn to sell somewhere reps are developed rather than churned, and your team has that reputation.
Best,
[Your Name]  |  [Phone]  |  [Email]

Dear [Hiring Manager],
I have hit or beaten quota in 11 of the last 12 quarters, including the two when the market gave me every excuse not to. I closed 2.4 million dollars in new business last year at an average deal size 40 percent above my team's, because I qualified hard and walked away from deals that were never going to close.
I sell complex products to senior buyers, which means I spend more time understanding a problem than pitching a solution. I have run full cycles from first call to signature, navigated procurement and security reviews without losing momentum, and built champions inside accounts who sold on my behalf when I was not in the room. I am disciplined in the CRM not for its own sake but because an honest pipeline is the only one you can forecast. I am disciplined about qualifying out early, because the deals that waste a quarter are usually the ones I should have walked away from in the first call, and learning to say no has done more for my numbers than any closing technique.
I am applying to [Company] because your product solves a problem I have watched cost my current customers real money, which makes the sale honest rather than a stretch. Your move into [named segment] opens exactly the kind of accounts where I do my best work.
I would welcome a conversation about your territory and targets, and I am happy to walk through how I built my largest deal. My CV is attached. I want to sell something I believe in to people I respect, and your product and your buyer give me both.
Regards,
[Your Name]  |  [Phone]  |  [Email]

hookDesc: building the engine, not just carrying the number
body:
Dear [CEO],
A VP of Sales is judged on whether the number survives their absence. The teams I have built keep performing because the system does the work, not the heroics. At [Company] I grew annual recurring revenue from 12 million to 47 million dollars in three years by rebuilding the sales motion, the hiring bar, and the forecasting discipline rather than by hiring my way out of every problem.
I have built and led teams of more than 40 across SDRs, account executives, and sales engineering. I rebuilt a comp plan that was rewarding the wrong behaviour, instituted a qualification framework that lifted win rates from 18 to 29 percent, and brought forecast accuracy within 5 percent so the board could plan with confidence. I hire for coachability and competitiveness in equal measure, and I am ruthless about removing the few who corrode a team's standards. I am honest with a board about the pipeline even when the honest number is uncomfortable, because a sales leader who manages the forecast rather than the business is borrowing against a quarter they will eventually have to repay. I also rebuilt onboarding so a new rep reached full productivity in eight weeks rather than sixteen, because a sales engine that cannot ramp people quickly cannot scale at all.
What interests me about [Company] is that you have strong product-market fit but a sales motion that has not yet scaled with it, which is a good problem and a familiar one to me. I have made that transition from founder-led selling to a repeatable engine twice.
I would welcome a conversation about your targets for the next two years and how I would build toward them. I am looking for a company with the product behind it and the ambition to scale the selling, and you have both.
Regards,
[Your Name]  |  [Phone]  |  [Email]

hookDesc: a landmark account won the hard way
body:
Dear [Hiring Manager],
It took 14 months to close [type of account] for a 3.1 million dollar annual contract, through three changes of sponsor and a competitive process we entered as the underdog. I won it by building relationships across the organisation rather than betting on a single champion, and that account is now a reference that has helped close two more like it.
I sell to the enterprise, where deals are long, buyers are many, and the work is mostly orchestration. I have carried and beaten quotas above 8 million dollars, built account plans that mapped genuine decision structures rather than org charts, and led the internal coordination of legal, security, and product that complex deals demand. I am comfortable in the boardroom and in the weeds, and I know that the deal is won or lost in the months before the proposal is sent. I am patient in a way enterprise selling requires, building the relationships across an organisation months before a proposal is ever written, because in deals this size the work that wins is mostly invisible and done long before the decision. I also build the internal business case that helps a champion sell upward when I am not in the room, because in enterprise deals the decision is usually made in meetings I will never attend.
I am applying to [Company] because your move upmarket requires a different sales motion than the one that got you here, and that transition is precisely what I do well. Your recent [named enterprise win] shows the ambition is real.
I would welcome a conversation about your largest target accounts and how I would approach them. I want to sell at the level where the work is mostly orchestration and patience, and your move upmarket creates exactly that.
Best regards,
[Your Name]  |  [Phone]  |  [Email]

hookDesc: a process fix with a hard number
body:
Dear [Hiring Manager],
At [Company] order-fulfilment errors were running at 4.2 percent and quietly costing the business in refunds and lost trust. I mapped the process end to end, found that most errors clustered at a single manual handoff, and redesigned it. Errors fell to 0.6 percent within a quarter, with no new headcount and no new software. The fix was attention, not budget.
I run operations by getting close to the work before I change it. Over six years I have cut average cycle time on a core process by 38 percent, built the reporting that let a leadership team see problems while they were still small, and managed teams of up to 30 through periods of real change without losing people or standards. I am comfortable with Lean tools but treat them as means, not religion, and I care most about whether the customer feels the difference. I spend time on the floor with the people doing the work before I change anything, because the best process improvement is usually one the team already half-knows and has simply never been asked about. I also built the simple weekly dashboard that let the leadership team see operational health at a glance, because most operational failures are visible weeks early to anyone actually looking.
What interests me about [Company] is that you are scaling quickly, which is exactly when good operations stop being optional and bad ones start to show. I have built the systems that let a company grow without the wheels coming off.
I would welcome a conversation about your current operational priorities. My CV is attached. I want to build operations somewhere growth makes the work matter more, and your stage tells me it does.
Regards,
[Your Name]  |  [Phone]  |  [Email]

Dear [Hiring Manager],
I was brought onto a 6 million dollar systems implementation that was four months late and had lost the confidence of its sponsor. I rebuilt the plan around what was actually true rather than what the status reports claimed, renegotiated scope with the steering group, and delivered the revised plan on time. Recovering a troubled project is harder than running a clean one, and it is the work I am most often asked to do.
Across more than 30 delivered projects I have managed budgets up to 10 million dollars, led cross-functional teams that did not report to me, and kept honest about risk when honesty was uncomfortable. I hold PMP and have worked in both waterfall and agile environments, and I have learned that method matters far less than clear ownership and a plan everyone actually believes. My status reports are short, true, and trusted. I keep status reports short and honest, because a project that looks green right up until it fails has a reporting problem on top of a delivery one, and I would rather deliver bad news early than explain a surprise late. I also keep a clear risk register that the steering group trusts, because the projects that fail quietly are almost always the ones where the risks were known but never written down.
What interests me about [Company] is the scale of your upcoming [named program], which carries the kind of cross-functional complexity where good project management is the difference between delivery and drift. I have run programs of that shape before.
I would welcome a conversation about the program and how I would approach its first 90 days. I want to run programs where the complexity is real and the delivery actually matters, and yours qualifies.
Best regards,
[Your Name]  |  [Phone]  |  [Email]

Dear [CEO],
A COO is hired to make growth survivable: to build the systems, the people, and the discipline that let a company scale without the quality that made it successful quietly degrading. I have done that through two periods of rapid growth, and I believe [Company] is entering one now.
At [Company A] I built the operational backbone that supported growth from 50 to 400 employees and from one market to nine, holding service quality steady throughout. At [Company B] I took on a business where growth had outrun its processes and rebuilt operations, supply chain, and the management rhythm, improving margin by 11 points over two years without layoffs. I run companies through clear metrics, capable people, and a cadence that surfaces problems early, and I partner with a CEO best when I own execution so they can own direction. I run the business through a management rhythm that surfaces problems while they are still small, because by the time an operational issue reaches the executive team as a crisis, the cheap moment to fix it has usually passed.
I lead operations as a service to the rest of the business, not a power base. That means building systems people want to use, hiring leaders better than me in their domains, and being the person in the executive team who says what is actually happening on the ground.
Your next phase of growth implies operational choices that are hard to reverse once made. I have a clear view of where the early investment matters most and where it would be wasted.
I would welcome a conversation about the role and the first hundred days. I am looking for a chief executive who wants to own direction while a partner owns execution, and your stage suggests you do.
Regards,
[Your Name]  |  [Phone]  |  [Email]

Dear [Hiring Manager],
When a primary supplier failed without warning in 2022, the contingency network I had spent the previous year building absorbed the shock. We kept every line running and lost no customer orders while competitors went short. The work that mattered was done quietly in the year before anyone needed it.
Over eight years in supply chain I have cut inventory carrying cost by 23 percent while improving on-time delivery to 98.5 percent, renegotiated supplier contracts that saved 4 million dollars a year without sacrificing reliability, and built the demand-planning model that brought forecast error down into single digits. I am fluent in the major ERP systems and in the analytics behind them, but I have learned that supplier relationships, the human ones, matter as much as any model when conditions turn. I invest in the supplier relationships during the calm periods, because the supplier who takes your call first in a shortage is the one you treated as a partner when you did not need anything from them. I also built the supplier scorecard that turned a vague sense of reliability into a number we could act on, because you cannot manage a supply base you cannot actually measure.
What interests me about [Company] is your push to regionalise a supply chain that has been heavily concentrated, which is the right strategic move and a genuinely hard operational one. I have led exactly that kind of diversification and know where the hidden costs sit.
I would welcome a conversation about your supply chain priorities. My CV is attached. I want to build resilient supply somewhere the strategy is ambitious and the execution is taken seriously, and yours is both.
Regards,
[Your Name]  |  [Phone]  |  [Email]

hookDesc: clinical judgement that changed an outcome
body:
Dear [Nurse Manager],
On a night shift last year I noticed a subtle change in a post-operative patient that the early-warning score had not yet flagged. I escalated, stayed with my assessment when it was questioned, and the patient was treated for an internal bleed that would likely have been missed until morning. The instinct behind that came from five years of paying close attention.
I am a registered nurse with five years on a busy surgical ward, where I have cared for high-acuity patients, precepted three newly qualified nurses, and served on the unit's falls-reduction group that brought our rate down by 31 percent over a year. I am calm in an emergency and careful in the routine, and I treat documentation and handover as patient safety rather than paperwork. My patients and their families know they can ask me anything. I treat handover and documentation as part of patient safety rather than paperwork, because the gaps between shifts are exactly where avoidable harm tends to happen, and a careful handover has prevented more than one. I also mentor newer nurses on exactly this kind of judgement, because the instinct to escalate early is learned, and a ward is only as safe as its least experienced member on a hard night.
I want to join [Hospital] because of your reputation for genuine investment in nursing development and your model of nurse-led care, which matches the kind of nurse I want to keep becoming. Your recent [named initiative or accreditation] reflects values I share.
I would welcome the chance to discuss how I would contribute to your team. My CV and references are attached. I want to nurse somewhere development is real rather than rhetorical, and your reputation tells me it is.
Sincerely,
[Your Name]  |  [Phone]  |  [Email]

Dear [Medical Director],
Over the past four years I have managed a complex caseload in [specialty] while leading a quality initiative that reduced 30-day readmissions on our service by 19 percent. The improvement came from a discharge protocol I designed with nursing and pharmacy colleagues, because the best clinical outcomes are rarely the work of a physician alone.
I completed my residency at [Institution] and am board certified in [specialty]. My practice balances evidence-based medicine with the time and attention patients actually need, and I have maintained strong patient-satisfaction scores without compromising clinical rigour. I have published [number] peer-reviewed papers, supervised residents and medical students, and contributed to the protocols my department now uses. I am comfortable with the administrative and quality work that modern medicine requires, and I see it as part of good care rather than a distraction from it. I see the administrative and quality work of modern medicine as part of good care rather than a distraction from it, because a protocol that prevents one avoidable readmission helps more patients than I could ever reach one at a time.
I am drawn to [Institution] because of your commitment to [named program or model of care] and your investment in clinical research alongside practice. The patient population you serve is one I have built real experience with, and I would value being part of a department that treats teaching and improvement as central.
I would welcome a conversation about how I would contribute clinically and to your wider mission. My CV and references are attached. I want to practise somewhere teaching and improvement are central rather than tolerated, and your department appears to be that place.
Sincerely,
[Your Name], MD  |  [Phone]  |  [Email]

Dear [Hiring Manager],
Emergency department wait times at [Facility] had reached a point where patients were leaving before being seen. I led a redesign of patient flow that brought the median wait from 94 minutes to 38 and cut the rate of patients leaving without treatment by more than half, working with clinical staff rather than imposing on them. In healthcare, an operational improvement is only real if it protects care.
Over a decade in healthcare administration I have managed budgets above 60 million dollars, led teams across clinical and support functions, and delivered operational change in an environment where the stakes are measured in patient outcomes, not just cost. I understand the regulatory landscape, I can read a clinical environment well enough to know which changes will be accepted and which will be quietly resisted, and I keep clinicians as partners because nothing improves without them. I keep clinicians as partners rather than subjects of a change, because an operational improvement they did not help design is one they will quietly work around, and in a hospital that workaround is measured in patient outcomes. I also built the reporting that let clinical leaders see operational pressure before it became a safety issue, because in a hospital the warning signs are almost always there before the incident is.
What interests me about [Organisation] is your move toward [named model, such as integrated or value-based care], which requires exactly the kind of operational and cultural change I have led before. Your recent [named initiative] signals the seriousness of that direction.
I would welcome a conversation about your priorities and how I would approach them. I want to lead operations somewhere clinicians are partners in change, and your direction suggests you understand that.
Regards,
[Your Name]  |  [Phone]  |  [Email]

Dear [Hiring Manager],
I grew my territory from the bottom quartile to first of 60 representatives over two years, not by more visits but by becoming a representative physicians actually wanted to see. I learned the clinical evidence well enough to have a real conversation, respected their time, and never oversold. Trust, in this role, compounds.
My background combines a science degree with the discipline of a structured sales process. I understand the clinical data behind the products I represent, I can hold my own in a conversation with a specialist, and I work within the compliance rules as a matter of course rather than constraint. I manage my territory like a business, with proper account planning and honest pipeline tracking, and I build the long relationships that turn a single prescription into a settled prescribing habit. I work strictly within the compliance rules as a matter of course, because the trust of a physician is built over years and lost in a single conversation that crosses a line, and that trust is the entire asset in this role. I also manage my territory with proper account planning rather than activity for its own sake, because in this field the relationship that compounds over years beats the visit count every time.
I am applying to [Company] because your [named product or therapeutic area] addresses a genuine gap I have seen physicians frustrated by, which makes the conversation easy to have honestly. Your pipeline in [area] suggests this is a place to build a long career rather than chase a quarter.
I would welcome a conversation about the territory and how I would build it. My CV is attached. I want to represent products I can stand behind to physicians I respect, and your pipeline gives me both.
Regards,
[Your Name]  |  [Phone]  |  [Email]

hookDesc: substantive contribution to a real matter
body:
Dear [Hiring Manager],
During a complex litigation matter at [Firm] I managed a document review of more than 80,000 items and built the chronology that became the backbone of the firm's case strategy. A supervising partner told me the chronology saved the team a week of work and surfaced two documents that changed how we argued the matter. I take quiet pride in being the person the attorneys can rely on.
Over five years I have supported litigation and corporate teams through filings, discovery, due diligence, and trial preparation, and I have learned the legal field's first rule: accuracy is everything, and a missed deadline is unforgivable. I am thoroughly comfortable with e-discovery platforms, case management systems, and the rules of court procedure, and I draft clean first versions of documents that need light revision rather than rebuilding. I keep matters organised so that nothing is ever lost and nothing is ever late. I keep matters organised so that nothing is ever lost and nothing is ever late, because in litigation a missed deadline is not an inconvenience but a malpractice exposure, and the attorneys I support should never have to wonder whether I have it handled.
I want to join [Firm] because of your reputation in [practice area] and because you give paralegals genuine substantive work rather than treating them as administrative support. The kind of matters you handle are the kind I want to grow within.
I would welcome the chance to discuss how I would support your team. My CV and references are attached. I want to do substantive legal work somewhere paralegals are trusted with it, and your reputation tells me you are that firm.
Sincerely,
[Your Name]  |  [Phone]  |  [Email]

hookDesc: a result and the reasoning behind it
body:
Dear [Hiring Partner],
In my third year at [Firm] I drafted the dispositive motion that ended a contract dispute in our client's favour without trial, saving them an estimated 18 months and a substantial legal spend. The partner trusted me with the argument because my earlier research memos had proven reliable under pressure. In this profession, trust is earned one careful document at a time.
I graduated from [Law School] and have spent the years since in [practice area], handling research, drafting, and increasingly the direct client contact that tells me whether an argument will actually persuade. I write clearly, which in law is rarer and more valuable than it should be, and I have learned to find the one case or clause that decides a matter rather than burying it in twenty that do not. I bill honestly and I meet deadlines, because the alternative is unthinkable. I bill honestly and I meet deadlines without exception, because the trust that lets a partner hand me an argument rather than a research task is built one reliable document at a time and cannot be rushed. I also learned to write a research memo a partner can act on directly, surfacing the one authority that decides a matter rather than burying it among twenty that merely touch it.
I am applying to [Firm] because of your standing in [practice area] and your reputation for developing associates into genuine lawyers rather than document machines. Your recent work on [named matter or area] is precisely the kind of practice I want to build.
I would welcome a conversation about your team and how I would contribute. My CV and a writing sample are attached. I want to become a real lawyer somewhere associates are developed rather than used, and your standing suggests you are that place.
Sincerely,
[Your Name]  |  [Phone]  |  [Email]

Dear [CEO],
A general counsel earns their place by helping the business move quickly and safely at the same time, rather than choosing between the two. I have built legal functions that executives treated as a partner in growth rather than a brake on it, and I believe [Company] is at the stage where that matters most.
Over fifteen years, including the last six as general counsel of a [size] company, I have led the legal side of two acquisitions and one financing round, built a compliance program that satisfied regulators without strangling the business, and managed litigation by settling what should be settled and fighting only what was worth fighting. I have sat on the executive team as a commercial thinker who happens to be a lawyer, and I have given the board the plain-spoken risk assessments they needed rather than the hedged ones that protect the adviser. I give the board the plain risk assessment they need rather than the hedged one that protects the adviser, because a general counsel who buries the real answer in qualifications is failing at the one thing the role exists to do.
I lead a legal function that the business wants to involve early, because involving us late is where the expensive mistakes happen. That comes from being fast, being commercial, and being willing to give a clear answer.
Your expansion into [named market or area] introduces regulatory and contractual complexity that rewards getting the legal foundation right early. I have built that foundation before in a comparable setting.
I would welcome a conversation about the role and your priorities for the next two years. I am looking for a chief executive who wants legal involved early as a partner in growth, and your stage suggests you do.
Regards,
[Your Name]  |  [Phone]  |  [Email]

Dear [Hiring Manager],
At [Company] voluntary turnover in the first year had reached 28 percent, and exit interviews pointed clearly at a weak onboarding experience. I redesigned the first 90 days around connection and clarity rather than paperwork, and first-year turnover fell to 11 percent within a year. Good HR work usually shows up as a number the rest of the business cares about.
Over five years as a generalist I have handled the full range, from recruitment and onboarding to employee relations, performance, and the difficult conversations that come with the role. I am trusted by both managers and staff because I am fair and consistent, and I keep confidence as an absolute. I know employment law well enough to keep the company safe and humane enough to remember that policy serves people, not the other way around. I am comfortable in the major HR systems and in the data that turns a vague sense of a problem into something you can actually fix. I hold confidence absolutely and treat both managers and staff fairly, because the moment people stop trusting that HR is even-handed, the function loses the only thing that makes it useful. I also built the simple people dashboard that turned a vague sense that something was wrong into a number a leadership team could act on, because culture problems are cheaper to fix when you can see them early.
What interests me about [Company] is your stated commitment to building a culture deliberately as you scale, which is the moment most companies lose the thing that made them special. I have helped protect culture through exactly that growth.
I would welcome a conversation about your people priorities. My CV is attached. I want to do people work somewhere culture is built deliberately, and your stated commitment tells me it is.
Regards,
[Your Name]  |  [Phone]  |  [Email]

Dear [Hiring Manager],
Most recruiting improves speed or quality and quietly sacrifices the other. At [Company] I brought average time to hire down from 52 days to 31 while improving the one-year retention of new hires by 14 points, by fixing the process rather than simply working it harder.
Over the years I have built recruiting functions from the ground up, hired across engineering, commercial, and leadership roles, and rebuilt interview processes that were filtering out good candidates for the wrong reasons. I treat candidate experience as a reflection of the company, because the people you do not hire talk too. I am comfortable with the data behind recruiting, from funnel conversion to source quality, and I use it to make the case for change rather than to decorate a report. I have learned that the best hires come from a clear scorecard and an honest pitch, not from volume. I treat the candidates we do not hire as carefully as the ones we do, because they talk, and a recruiting process that leaves good people feeling disrespected costs the company hires it will never even know it lost. I also rebuilt the interview scorecards so hiring decisions rested on evidence rather than impression, which did as much for the quality of our hires as any sourcing improvement.
What interests me about [Company] is the scale of hiring ahead of you, which is the point where a recruiting function either becomes a real advantage or a constant bottleneck. I have built the kind that becomes an advantage.
I would welcome a conversation about your hiring plans and how I would approach them. I want to build recruiting somewhere it is treated as an advantage rather than a bottleneck, and your hiring plans give me that chance.
Regards,
[Your Name]  |  [Phone]  |  [Email]

hookDesc: culture and performance as one agenda
body:
Dear [CEO],
The companies that scale well treat culture and performance as the same agenda rather than competing ones. I have built people functions that made that true, and I believe [Company] is at the inflection point where it matters most.
At [Company A] I led the people strategy through growth from 300 to 1,400 employees, building the leadership development, the performance framework, and the deliberate culture work that kept engagement high while the company doubled twice. At [Company B] I rebuilt a function that had become purely administrative into a genuine strategic partner, and we improved retention of senior talent by 22 percent through better management rather than better perks. I sit on the executive team as a business leader who owns the people agenda, and I give the CEO and board honest reads on culture, capability, and risk. I build managers who can actually lead, because culture is created and destroyed by managers far more than by any program HR runs, and the highest-leverage thing a people function can do is raise the floor of everyday management.
I lead by building managers who can lead, because culture is created or destroyed by managers far more than by any program HR runs. My job is to make the right behaviour easy and the wrong behaviour visible.
Your next phase of growth will test whether the culture that built the company can survive its scale. I have protected and evolved culture through exactly that test, and I have a clear view of where to start.
I would welcome a conversation about the role and the first hundred days. I am looking for a chief executive who treats culture and performance as one agenda, and your stage suggests you are.
Regards,
[Your Name]  |  [Phone]  |  [Email]

hookDesc: real work with a real result
body:
Dear [Creative Director],
For a local business with almost no budget I rebuilt a brand identity that had not changed in fifteen years, from logo to packaging to social templates. Within six months the owner told me foot traffic from younger customers had clearly grown, and the work became the centrepiece of my portfolio. I learned that good design solves a business problem, not just an aesthetic one.
I am completing my degree in Graphic Design at [University], where I have built a portfolio across branding, editorial, and digital work, and I freelance to keep my skills tied to real clients with real opinions. I am fluent in the full Adobe suite and in Figma, I understand the fundamentals of typography and layout rather than just imitating trends, and I take feedback as direction rather than criticism. I care about the craft, the kerning that no one consciously notices, and I work fast without letting quality slip. I take feedback as direction rather than criticism, because the fastest way to grow as a junior designer is to want the honest note more than the reassurance, and I would rather hear what is wrong now than discover it in front of a client.
I want to join [Studio] because your work has a clear point of view rather than chasing whatever is current, and your recent [named project] showed restraint that is rare and that I admire. I want to learn from people who design with intent.
My portfolio is at [URL]. I would welcome the chance to talk through the thinking behind a few pieces. I want to learn from people who design with intent rather than to a trend, and your work tells me you are those people.
Best,
[Your Name]  |  [Phone]  |  [Email]  |  [Portfolio URL]

Dear [Hiring Manager],
I rewrote a single onboarding screen and watched completion rise by 23 percent. No new design, no engineering, just clearer words that respected the reader. That is the part of this craft I find most satisfying: the proof that language is not decoration but function.
Over the years I have written across the full range, from brand campaigns that needed to make people feel something to interface copy that needed to make a confusing moment simple. I have built voice-and-tone guidelines that kept large teams sounding like one company, written the error messages and empty states that most teams ignore and users quietly resent, and partnered with designers and product managers closely enough that the words and the design were never an afterthought to each other. I write tight, I cut my own favourite lines without sentiment, and I know that the best copy is usually the copy no one notices. I cut my own favourite lines without sentiment, because the reader did not come for my cleverness, they came to understand something quickly, and the writer's job is to get out of their way. I also built the writing tests and review process that kept a growing team's work consistent, because voice is easy to define and hard to hold across a dozen writers under deadline.
What interests me about [Company] is that you clearly treat writing as part of the product rather than a layer applied at the end, which your recent [named feature or rebrand] made obvious. That is the environment where a writer does their best work.
My portfolio is at [URL]. I would welcome a conversation about your team and how I would contribute. I want to write somewhere the words are treated as part of the product, and your recent work makes clear you do.
Best,
[Your Name]  |  [Phone]  |  [Email]  |  [Portfolio URL]

Dear [Hiring Manager],
The campaign I am proudest of did not win an award. It took a struggling [product] and made it the category leader within two years, lifting sales by 64 percent on a budget smaller than the incumbent's. Creative work earns its place when it moves the business, and that is the standard I hold my teams to.
Over twelve years I have led creative teams across advertising and in-house, built and protected brands through good and bad markets, and learned to defend a brave idea to a nervous client without losing either the idea or the client. I hire people more talented than me and give them room, I keep the work honest to a clear strategy rather than to my own taste, and I have built creative cultures that people did not want to leave. I can read a brief for the business problem underneath it, which is where the best ideas actually come from. I hire people more talented than me and give them genuine room, because a creative director whose teams only ever produce the director's own ideas has built a bottleneck rather than a department. I also build the kind of creative culture people do not want to leave, because in this industry the work is only ever as good as the talent you can keep, and talent stays where it is trusted.
What interests me about [Company] is that you are at a moment that calls for genuine creative reinvention rather than refinement, and your recent [named work] suggested an appetite for that. I have led brands through exactly that kind of reset.
I would welcome a conversation about where you want the work to go and how I would lead it there. I want to lead creative somewhere the work is held to a commercial standard as well as a craft one, and your appetite for reinvention suggests you do.
Regards,
[Your Name]  |  [Phone]  |  [Email]  |  [Portfolio URL]

hookDesc: visual direction with a measurable effect
body:
Dear [Hiring Manager],
I led the visual rebrand of [product or company], and in the year after launch we saw the kind of result that is rare to attribute to design directly: a measurable lift in perceived quality in brand tracking and a 29 percent rise in engagement on owned channels. The direction worked because it was built on a clear idea, not on a moodboard.
My background is in art direction across campaigns, digital products, and brand systems, and I have learned to hold a consistent vision across a dozen executions and a dozen opinions. I direct photographers, illustrators, and designers in a way that gets their best work rather than my work through their hands, and I am as comfortable with the craft details as with the big idea. I keep the work tied to strategy, because beautiful work that does not serve the brief is just expensive decoration. I direct other makers in a way that gets their best work rather than my work through their hands, because the point of art direction is to multiply talent, not to funnel everything through a single taste. I also hold the visual system consistent across every execution and every opinion, because a brand that looks like ten different companies across its channels has no identity at all.
What interests me about [Company] is that your visual identity has real equity but has started to feel dated against where the category is heading, which is a delicate problem and the kind I do well. Your recent [named project] hinted at the direction.
My portfolio is at [URL]. I would welcome a conversation about the work and how I would direct it. I want to direct somewhere the visual identity genuinely matters to the business, and your equity in it tells me it does.
Best regards,
[Your Name]  |  [Phone]  |  [Email]  |  [Portfolio URL]

Dear [Hiring Manager],
In a university consulting project for a regional retailer I built the analysis that showed their expansion plan was targeting the wrong cities. The client changed three of their five planned locations based on our recommendation, and the two original sites they kept have since underperformed the ones we suggested. Being able to change a real decision with clear analysis is why this work appeals to me.
I graduate from [University] in [Month] with a degree in [field] and a strong quantitative record. I think in structures, breaking a vague problem into parts that can actually be solved, and I am comfortable building the financial and operational models that turn an opinion into a case. I work well under the deadline pressure consulting is known for, I write and present clearly, and I have learned to hold a recommendation lightly enough to change it when the data does, which I suspect matters more than any single technical skill. I hold a recommendation lightly enough to change it when the data does, which I have come to think matters more than any single modelling skill, because the analyst who falls in love with their first answer is the one who misleads the client.
I am applying to [Firm] because of your reputation in [practice area] and because you put analysts on substantive client work early rather than on the bench. The kind of problems you solve for clients are exactly the ones I want to learn to solve well.
