My CV is attached. I would welcome the chance to discuss how I think through a problem. I would rather earn the role by showing how I work through one of your real problems than by listing credentials, and I am glad to do that.
Sincerely,
[Your Name]  |  [Phone]  |  [Email]

hookDesc: a recommendation the client actually implemented
body:
Dear [Hiring Manager],
Most consulting recommendations end up in a drawer. The one I am proudest of did not: a cost-and-growth strategy for a 400 million dollar manufacturer that the client implemented in full, delivering 38 million dollars in annual margin improvement over two years. The difference was that I built it with the client's own people rather than presenting it to them, so it was theirs to own.
Over the years I have led engagement teams across strategy, operations, and transformation, sold and delivered work that clients renewed, and learned that the hard part of consulting is rarely the analysis. It is getting an organisation to actually change. I am strong on the quantitative side, but my real value is translating a sound recommendation into something a client will live with after we leave. I develop the junior people on my teams deliberately, because the quality of the work depends on them. I build recommendations with a client's own people rather than presenting to them, because the analysis is the easy part and getting an organisation to actually change is the hard one, and change that is imposed rather than owned rarely survives contact with the real world. I also develop the junior people on my teams deliberately, because the quality of the analysis depends on them, and a senior consultant who hoards the interesting work builds nothing that lasts.
What interests me about [Firm] is your model of staying with clients through implementation rather than stopping at the recommendation, which is where the value actually gets created. That matches how I have always believed this work should be done.
I would welcome a conversation about your practice and the clients I would serve. I want to consult somewhere we stay through implementation rather than stopping at the slide, and your model matches that conviction.
Regards,
[Your Name]  |  [Phone]  |  [Email]

hookDesc: a design that was actually built
body:
Dear [Hiring Manager],
My final-year team designed a low-cost water-filtration unit for off-grid communities, and unlike most student projects, it was built and field-tested. The working prototype processed 40 litres an hour at a material cost under 30 dollars, and an NGO is now adapting the design for a pilot. Seeing a design leave the page and do its job taught me what engineering is actually for.
I graduate from [University] in [Month] with a degree in Mechanical Engineering and a strong record in design and thermodynamics. I am proficient in SolidWorks and in the simulation tools that let you fail on a screen before you fail in metal, and a summer placement at [Company] gave me real exposure to manufacturing constraints, the gap between an elegant design and one a factory can actually make. I am methodical, I document properly, and I would rather ask a question early than discover an expensive mistake late. I document my work properly and would rather ask a question early than discover an expensive mistake late, because in engineering the cost of a misunderstanding grows quietly with every stage it survives undetected. I also learned during my placement how much of real engineering is the constraint you did not anticipate, the tolerance or the supplier lead time that turns an elegant design into one that has to be reworked.
I want to join [Company] because you build [type of product] that solves real problems rather than chasing specifications for their own sake, and your recent [named product] is a clear example. I want to learn engineering from people who build things that matter.
My CV and a portfolio of design projects are attached. I would welcome the chance to discuss them. I want to learn engineering from people who build things that matter, and your products tell me you are those people.
Sincerely,
[Your Name]  |  [Phone]  |  [Email]

Dear [Hiring Manager],
I led the structural design for a [type] project worth 45 million dollars, on difficult ground that two earlier proposals had judged unbuildable within budget. The foundation solution I developed brought it within budget and, more importantly, it was delivered with a clean safety record across 18 months of construction. In this field, the project that goes up safely and quietly is the one you should be proudest of.
Over twelve years I have taken projects from concept through detailed design to construction, signed off as the responsible engineer on work where the calculations carry real consequence, and coordinated the architects, contractors, and authorities whose agreement a project depends on. I am a chartered engineer fluent in the relevant codes and software, and I have learned that the discipline of checking, and being checked, is not bureaucracy but the foundation the whole profession rests on. I treat the discipline of checking and being checked as the foundation the profession rests on rather than as bureaucracy, because in structural work the calculation no one reviewed is exactly the one that should worry everybody. I also coordinate closely with the architects and contractors whose agreement a project depends on, because the most elegant structural solution is worthless if it cannot actually be built on the site as drawn.
What interests me about [Company] is your portfolio of [type] projects and your reputation for technical ambition matched with genuine rigour. The complexity of your recent [named project] is exactly the kind of work I want to keep doing.
I would welcome a conversation about your upcoming projects and how I would contribute. My CV is attached. I want to do technically ambitious work somewhere rigour is non-negotiable, and your portfolio suggests it is.
Regards,
[Your Name]  |  [Phone]  |  [Email]

hookDesc: a clear thesis on the company's next chapter
body:
Dear [Board Chair],
I have read [Company]'s recent results and public strategy closely, and I believe the central question is not whether the market is there but whether the organisation is built to capture it. I have led two companies through exactly that kind of gap between opportunity and execution, and I have a specific thesis on how I would close it here.
At [Company A] I took the business from 200 million to 750 million dollars in revenue over six years, entered three new markets, and built a leadership team strong enough that the company kept compounding after I left, which I consider the truest measure of the work. At [Company B] I led a turnaround that returned the company to profitability within 18 months without sacrificing the long-term investments that mattered, and rebuilt the trust of a board and a market that had begun to lose patience. I lead through clarity of strategy, discipline of capital, and the relentless development of people, and I have learned that culture is the only durable advantage. I have learned that culture is the only advantage a competitor cannot copy, and that the chief executive's deepest responsibility is to the institution's long-term health rather than to any single quarter's applause.
I would treat this role as a stewardship rather than a position: to leave the company stronger, more capable, and more valuable than I found it, and to build the team that carries it beyond my tenure.
I have a clear view of the first hundred days and where the real leverage sits, and I would welcome the chance to test it against the board's own thinking. I would treat the role as a stewardship of the institution rather than a position within it, and I would welcome testing that conviction against the board's.
Regards,
[Your Name]  |  [Phone]  |  [Email]

hookDesc: the specific contribution to this board
body:
Dear [Chair],
I am writing regarding the non-executive position on [Company]'s board. I will be direct about why I believe I would add value: your stated priorities for the next phase, particularly [named priority such as international expansion or digital transformation], align closely with the two areas where I have the deepest operating experience and the clearest pattern recognition.
My executive career, including [number] years as [former role] of a [size and type] company, gave me direct accountability for the decisions a board oversees rather than merely reviews: capital allocation, succession, risk, and the difficult judgement calls that do not have clean answers. I currently serve on [number] other boards, where I chair the [committee] committee, and I have learned the discipline that distinguishes a good non-executive: to support and challenge an executive team without trying to run the company, and to ask the question in the room that everyone is privately thinking. I understand the line a good non-executive holds, supporting and challenging an executive team without trying to run the company, because a board that drifts into management does the company a quieter but real disservice.
I am drawn to [Company] specifically because the challenges ahead are ones where I have genuinely useful scar tissue, not generic experience. I would bring an independent perspective, relevant networks, and a commitment to the long-term health of the company over short-term comfort.
I would welcome a conversation with you and the nominations committee about whether my experience fits what the board needs now. I would welcome the chance to discuss whether my experience matches what the board actually needs at this point, rather than what a generic profile suggests.
Regards,
[Your Name]  |  [Phone]  |  [Email]

Dear [Hiring Manager],
The program I built reached 12,000 young people over three years, but the number I hold onto is different: an independent evaluation found that participants were 40 percent more likely to be in education or work a year on. In this sector it is easy to measure activity and hard to measure change, and I have always insisted on measuring the change.
Over a decade in the nonprofit sector I have designed and run programs, managed budgets above 4 million dollars across restricted and unrestricted funding, and built the relationships with funders that turn a one-year grant into a multi-year partnership. I have led teams through the particular pressure of this work, where the need is greater than the resources and the temptation is to burn people out for the cause. I protect my teams while holding them to a real standard, because the people we serve deserve our best, not just our good intentions. I can write the honest evaluation that a funder trusts more than an optimistic one. I protect my teams from burning out for the cause while still holding them to a real standard, because the people we serve deserve our best work rather than just our good intentions, and an exhausted team delivers neither. I also built the honest evaluation framework that funders came to trust, because in this sector the temptation to report the encouraging anecdote over the real outcome is constant, and resisting it is what earns a multi-year partnership.
What draws me to [Organisation] is that you measure your work seriously rather than hiding behind anecdotes, and your recent [named program or report] reflected exactly that honesty. The population you serve is one I have spent my career alongside.
I would welcome a conversation about your programs and the impact you are working toward. I want to do mission work somewhere impact is measured honestly, and your reporting tells me you are that organisation.
Sincerely,
[Your Name]  |  [Phone]  |  [Email]
